texts.orig: Membrane  science and polymer  science have  grown synergistically over  the past  thirty years. Clearly, today's impressive set  of  membrane  processes and products could  not  exist without the  sophisticated array of  polymers  provided by synthetic  hemists. 1-6 At the  same time, the  membrane  field has  provided  a strong  driving force  and justification for  many  fundamental  studies of  the polymer solid  state  and polymer solution thermodynamics. 7  ~ 2  Indeed,  the theory still lags practice in  membrane formation, and  in  tailoring the  properties of  selective layers responsible for  the  operation of  membranes.  The continued evolution of  membrane  and polymer  science will  undoubtably lead  to  more sophisticated membranes  able  to achieve  increasingly difficult separations, someday possibly rivaling natural membranes.

texts.orig: The present discussion provides an update  on current theory  and practice involving separations of  gases and  liquids with  polymeric membranes where  the controlling permeation  mechanism for  separation involves a thermodynamic solution and a kinetic diffusion process. In  a practical sense, several levels of structure determine the  ultimate performance  of  a membrane.  2  The principal focus  of  this discussion is  on the  most basic levels of  structure, involving the primary  chemical segment  composition and  the  steric relationships between  the segmental repeat units comprising the  selective polymer  skin. Clearly, changes in  these basic levels of  chemical and steric factors can  impact  additional structure levels involving skin morphology  and  the  overall relationship between  skin and porous  support in  an asymmetric  membrane. These most basic  levels of structure, therefore, are  critically important and lie at  the  interface between polymer science and the  practical field of  membrane  technology. A  "new" polymeric membrane material exists when a novel  molecular-level environment is  generated, through  which the transport process  occurs.  This definition includes chemically identical polymers  that have  been  processed in  sufficiently different manners to  yield different molecular  scale discriminating properties due  to  segmental orientation or  aggregation. Also  included are  miscible polymer blends, and antiplasticized polymers*  whose fundamental motions  and  packing arrangements have  been  altered relative to  the  unblended  material.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three means of  achieving new molecular  scale  environments in  the  dense selective skin  of  a membrane can  be  envisioned: (i) chemical  alteration of  the backbone  of  the  constituent polymer  by  polymerization or  copolymerization of new monomers (ii) physical alteration by  blending of  macromolecular or  micromolecular  species with a given  polymer,  and (iii) physical alteration of  the selective skin  by control of  processing protocols such  as  applied stresses and cooling rates during  membrane formation.

texts.orig: Literature exists concerning the  effects on sorption and transport caused  by factors such  as  orientation and  crystallinity for  the case  of  dense  films, j3"14 The effects of  such  factors in  influencing the  actual behavior of  the  selective skins of asymmetric  membranes  are likely to be similar to those  observed  in these carefully controlled studies with  dense  films. Usually, sufficient freedom  does not  exist to  independently control these factors while  still giving the  membrane its necessary higher  order  structure required to  physically withstand a large differential pressure drop with  negligible flow  resistance through  its  porous support structure. Although  the  effects of  skin  orientation and  aggregation due to  processing will not  be  emphasized  here, they  can  be  extremely important, t5 and the  reader is encouraged  to  consider literature dealing with  these phenomena 7-j5 to  complement  the  current discussion.

texts.orig: In ultrafiltration and microfiltration, the  primary  mechanism of  separation involves a  hydrodynamic  sieving process, rather than  a  segmental scale solutiondiffusion process. Since  the  focus  of  the  present review  is  on phenomena that depend on the  micromolecular environment  of  a polymer,  ultrafiltration and microfiltration processes will  not  be covered. Material selection criteria, such as thermal  stability and resistance to environmental attack  by stream  components,  are important  for  both solution-diffusion membranes  and hydrodynamic sieving membranes. The broad range  of  materials to be discussed should,  therefore, be of  considerable interest to individuals in all  areas  of membrane separations.

texts.orig: The range  of  possible membrane structures is  broad, but  it is  useful to  classify these structures as  being  either "dense"  or  "asymmetric". Refinements of  each category are  encountered in  different applications. Dense membranes used in chlor-alkali and electrodialysis ion-exchange typically have little gradation in structure between  the  two  faces; however,  they  may be  supported on a  fabric for mechanical stability. Moreover,  the  two  faces of  the  active membrane layer may have different functional groups to optimize  interactions with the external phase.  In  the  case  of  chlor-alkali membranes, for  example,  one face  may have a  carboxylic acid  functionality while  the  other has  a  sulfonic acid  functionality. '6 This  chemical  difference is  significant in  providing optimum permeability and permselectivity in  actual chlor-alkali use.

texts.orig: Formation  of  a defect-free skin  on an asymmetric  membrane  is  difficult to achieve. Consequently, as  shown in  Fig.  1,  a  post-treatment was developed for polysulfone membranes for  gas  separation application. In this procedure  the imperfect membrane is  effectively "caulked" with  silicone rubber  as  a  means of circumventing this problem. ~9-z~  The separation process in  caulked  membranes still takes  place  in  the  dense  outside skin  of  the  polysulfone, with  the  silicone rubber simply  preventing gross nonselective viscous flow  through defects. In  the ideal case, the  silicone rubber  resides strictly in  the  porous  defects in  the  skin, however,  in  practice a small  amount presumably  also exists on the  nondefective regions  of  the  membrane  surface. texts.orig: More or  less uniformly microporous  membranes,  with  or  without porous skins, can  be  formed  by  thermally induced  phase  separation processes. These structures are  useful for filtration-type separations and  also find applications as supports for  thin composite  membranes discussed below.  Rather  difficult to process semicrystalline materials such  as  polyolefins can  be  formed  into porous membranes using this technology. 23'24 These  materials, however,  have  not  been useful in  nonporous  skinned solution-diffusion separation membranes,  so  they will not  be  treated further in  the  present review.

texts.orig: The last membrane type  shown in  Fig. 1  is the  so-called thin-film composite. Such  membranes can  be  produced  by  dip-coating a  pore  free selective polymer layer on a  porous  substrate. 25  Alternatively the  pore  free layer may be  formed on the  membrane  surface by reacting monomer  "a"  present in  an external aqueous  phase  with  monomer  "b".  Monomer  "b",  originally imbibed  in  the pores of  the  support, is supplied by  diffusion to  the interface. 26'27 This  technique has  been  used  to  form  an  intractable crosslinked skin that could not  be  made by the  simple  "integrally-skinned" asymmetric formation scheme  in  Fig.  1.

texts.orig: Because  of  the  relative thinness of  the  separating layer responsible for  the actual  permselection process,  membranes  must have good  resistance to attack by stream  components.  Formation  of  even microscopic flaws  in  the dense  skin  can  lead  to  viscous flow  across the  membrane which  is  fatal for solution/diffusion separations. Semicrystalline polymers offer  a means  of improving  the  chemical resistance of  a membrane material but  this improvement in  resistance is  accompanied  by a serious reduction in  membrane productivity in  solution/diffusion separations as  well  as  increased difficulties in membrane formation processing noted  above.  A  simple  approach  for  dealing with  small amounts  of  aggressive components  in  process streams is  to  remove them in  a pretreatment step. For instance, removal  of  chlorine with  sodium sulfite (Na2  SO3)  or  sodium  metabisulfite (Na2  S~  O4) for reverse osmosis  separation or  condensation of  aromatic hydrocarbons or  ammonia in  gas  separations are additional expenses that  must be considered in membrane  processes. The cost and  effectiveness of  the  simple  treatment procedures noted  here  must be  weighed  against the use  of more  exotic materials. When simple pretreatments are  possible, the  expense  associated with  the use  of  exotic materials is  typically not justified. 28'29  On  the other  hand, in applications  uch as chlor-alkali cells, where the  major  stream  components  are  very  aggressive, the  use  of  a premium  material  such as sulfonated perfluoro polymers (e.g.  Nation ® Perfluorinated Membranes) is justified.

texts.orig: To guide  research in  tailoring the  environment through  which transport occurs in  a  membrane,  it is  important to  "calibrate" one's thinking concerning the length scales that control the process of  permeation. For  gases  the minimum critical dimension of  the  molecule typically ranges from  2.5-5 ~.  As shown in Table 1,  precise separation of  different gases  on the  basis of  size  involves discrimination between  dimensions with  differences as  small as  0.2-0.5 A. This scale of  dimensions is clearly much smaller than  the  typical  ength of  even  the single repeat unit of  a  polymer. For  liquid penetrants, the scales of  sizes and  size differences are  probably less than  an  order of  magnitude larger than  that noted for  gases, so the  primary  structure of  the  polymer  molecule  and the  subtle motions  within a repeat unit itself become important in  the  separation of  the subtly different permeating components.

texts.orig: Moreover,  for both  gas  and  liquid penetrants, thermodynamic  interactions between the  polymer  and sorbed  penetrants are  dependent  on the  detailed chemical structure of  the polymer  repeat unit. Therefore, for solution-diffusion separations, the  primary chemical structure of  the  polymer  strongly influences both  the  thermodynamics and kinetics of  the  permeation process. However, additional factors besides the chemical composition of the polymer comprising the membrane impact  its performance in  a  separation application. texts.orig: Structure level four  includes both gradations in density from the  dense skin  to  the  porous  support in  an integrally skinned  membrane and the  interfacial bonding  characteristics of  the skin  to  the porous  support in  a  composite membrane. Additional refinements and sublevels can  be  incorporated into the discussion to  account for  factors such  as  penetrant clustering or  percolation corridor formation in  membranes such  as  Nation ® Perfluorinated Membranes which  arise due  to  complex  incompatible  lements present in  the  same chain. 3 ~ In  the case  of Nation ® Perfluorinated Membranes,  for instance, a  subtle balance must be maintained between the  physical strength, the  ionic selectivity and electrical resistivity of  the  membrane. Control of  the  detailed phase-separated morphology  provides an  additional adjustable variable for optimization of  these three somewhat disparate property requirements.

texts.orig: As one might  expect, the  use  of  pure  component permeabilities in  mixed penetrant feed  situations is  most  appropriate for  gaseous  penetrants because their low  solubilities cause little modification of  the  inherent membrane properties. Moreover,  in  the  absence of  applied electrical fields, even  aqueous  ionic systems  can often  be treated approximately by using  effectively constant permeabilities of  the water  and  salt components  if the  ionic concentrations are not  too  high. 33  Complications in  using pure  component  permeability ratios are encountered in  the case of  gases such  as CO2, SO2,  and  NH3, whose  high  critical temperatures and strong  tendencies to interact with  polymers  can lead  to plasticization of  the membrane material. Moreover, for ionic systems, problems can  result when ionic concentrations are  significantly different between  comparison cases, since  differences in  water  sorption levels in  the  membrane can affect component  partition coefficients and  diffusion coefficients.

texts.orig: The mobility selectivity term  in  eq.  (4)  is  based  on the  inherent ability of polymer  matrices to  function as  molecular sizeand  shape-selective media.  This ability is  governed  primarily by such  factors as  chain  backbone  rigidity and intersegmental packing (e.g. structure level two  noted  above). An illustration of the  higher inherent mobility selectivity of  glassy vs  rubbery matrices is given  in Fig. 3  where  the diffusion coefficients of  different components  at infinite dilution are  represented. 34  The van  der  Waals  volume  has  been  used  in  the  correlation as  a size measure,  since the  more desirable zeolite sieving diameters are  not available for  all of  the  penetrants in  Fig.  3.  The inherent mobility selectivity [Di/Dj] reflected by  the steepness of  the  slope of  the lines between  any  two  given penetrants in  the  figure clearly favors the  glassy polyvinyl chloride material.

texts.orig: Since  rubbery  polymers  have mobility  selectivities that  are  very  low in comparison  to  glassy polymers, plasticization of a glassy material into  a  condition closer to  the  rubbery state reduces the  mobility selectivity considerably. Under these conditions, the  overall permselectivity of  the  membrane will drop sharply, because strong compensating increases in  solubility selectivity generally do not accompany the  plasticization process. Solubility selectivity is determined on  the  basis of  interactions between  the  penetrants and  polymer  composing  the membrane for  both  liquid and gaseous  systems. 35  For  gases, the  relative condensibilities of  the  penetrants, characterized by  their critical temperatures, also play  an important role.

texts.orig: Originally, the  cellulose esters were chosen for  gas separation applications because  earlier work on reverse osmosis  could  be  easily adapted  to  form dry asymmetric  structures using  solvent-exchange dehydration. The processing ease and  availability ofthe  cellulose polymers  have  continued to  make this family of materials important commercial  competitors in  the  first three  gas  separation areas listed above. The most  important groups  of the  modified cellulosics are  the cellulose acetates, ethyl cellulose and their derivatives. This  family  includes a spectrum  of  materials composed of  varying  degrees of  acetylation of  the  OH groups  on cellulose (see  Fig.  4).  These materials have been considered by others 36'37 and,  although  they  are  important materials, they  will not  be  considered  further except  as a  comparison point  for  more  recent  advanced materials.

texts.orig: Shown in  Table  3 are  the  structures for  a family of  polyimides along  with the  structures of  the  constituent monomers.*  Gas permeabilities and  permselectivities of  various gas  pairs for  the  PMDA-based  polyimides are  plotted in Figs  5(a-d). The solid lines correspond to  typical selectivity, e,  vs  permeability behavior  achievable with  commercially available glassy  polymers  listed in Table  4.  Permeabilities and  selectivities for rubbery polymers listed in  Table  5  are also presented in  Figs  5(a-d). Points that fall to  the  upper  right of  the  line are attractive candidates, while  those in  the lower  left (most  rubbery polymers) are less desirable. The selectivity data  can  be  rationalized in  terms  of  the  relative sizes of  the  various penetrants as  characterized by  their kinetic diameters taken from  zeolite data, shown above  in  Table  I. In  each  polymer  the  permeability of helium  (smallest kinetic diameter) is  the  highest, followed  by those  of  the progressively larger penetrants. As illustrated by  the  plots in  Figs  5(a-d), in  all cases  except for  the O2/N 2 system  the  rigid chain  polyimides (PMDA-ODA, PMDA-MDA, PMDA-IPDA, and  Ultem  ® polyetherimide) fall significantly to the  upper  right of  the  lines representing the  typical behavior for commercially glassy polymers. The He/CH4 point for  ULTEM ® is not  plotted in  Figs 5  and 6 since  it is  so  far  off scale, but  its selectivity reported in  Table  4 is  clearly impressive.

texts.orig: Rigorous  analysis of  the  performance  of  any material for  a given  gas  pair requires both  solubility and diffusivity data  to  factor the  permeability into  its constituent parts. For these  rigid imides  with  hindered  segmental  mobility, however,  solubility differences are  of  second  order importance in  explaining the general trends observed. For  instance, as  shown in  Table  6,  the  permselectivity of  CO2  relative to  CH4  is due  to  both  solubility and  diffusivity factors; however, on the  basis of  diffusivity differences alone, all three polyimides are  selective for CO2 over CH 4 by a factor of  8-12.  The solubility differences play  a small  role in  comparison  to  the  powerful  diffusivity selectivity effects for  this gas  pair. Similar conclusions are  anticipated for  other gas  pairs; however,  presently solubility data  required to  demonstrate these ideas unambiguously  are  unavailable.

texts.orig: It  is  anticipated that as  the  central moiety  of  the  diamine  is  varied to  progressively bulkier groups [-  Othrough - CH2  -  to - C(CH3)2 -] both intrasegmental mobility and intersegmental packing  become more restricted. Wide angle  X-ray diffraction measurements  allow  calculations of  d-spacings from  Bragg's Law which  can  be  used  as  a  measure  of  the  average intersegmental distance between the  centers of  adjacent molecules. 44 Both the  macroscopic densities and d-spacings reported in  Table  3 reflect the  increased resistance to packing  as  one  moves to  a more bulky  central moiety  in  the  diamine  residue. The permeability of  each  gas  tends  to  increase significantly as  the  polymer  is varied from  PMDA-ODA to  PMDA-MDA to  PMDA-IPDA. Furthermore, the mobility selectivity for  the  CO2/CH4  pair (0.5 A difference in  kinetic diameter) decreases as  packing  resistance increases. This  suggests that the  increase in  the d-spacing from ODA to  IPDA  was not  offset by a sufficient increase in  resistance to rotation about the isopropylidene linkage  to maintain  equivalent size  selectivity. Hence, an undesirable change results in the  distribution of transiently opening  gaps  (e.g. free volume)  controlling the  mobility selectivity

texts.orig: Consider,  for  example Table 7 which shows that  significant increases in permeability are  observed  for  the  6FDA-ODA material as  compared to  the PMDA-ODA material for  each  of  the  five penetrants. In  fact, the  permselectivities of  He (or  H2) and CO2 over CH 4  are  not  decreased at  all in  spite of  a six  to  eight fold  increase in  permeabilities due to  replacement of  PMDA with 6FDA.  As in  the  previous case, diffusion coefficient differences between  the polymers  are  expected  to  be primarily responsible for  the  differences in  the observed  permeabilities of  any  given  penetrant in  the  two polymers. Previous studies have  shown little variation in  the  relative solubility of  most  gas  pairs in different polymers  unless moieties are  introduced that tend  to  interact strongly with  one  of  the members of  the gas  pair. 2°'29'35 Strong  specific interactions are  not anticipated between  any  of the  gases studied and  either the  PMDA or  the  6FDA unit, so  the  solubility selectivity data  for each  gas  pair is expected to  be  similar in these two  polymers. For  instance, while  perfluorination flow  molecular weight hydrocarbons  results in higher  absolute 02 solubility, the  OdN2 solubility selectivity is  essentially unaffected. 2°  For  the  present polyimide cases, the  similarity of  the  overall permselectivities also suggests a  corresponding similarity in diffusivity selectivity for the  various gas  pairs between  these two  polymers. Such a trend can be  rationalized in  terms  of  the  principles introduced above.

texts.orig: In the  PMDA-ODA polymer,  the  PMDA section is  fiat, "packable" and rigid. A PMDA chain  segment  that directly neighbors another PMDA segment should  be  much less able  to  execute motions  leading to  gas  molecule diffusion than  when such  a  PMDA segment  neighbors an ODA segment  in  the  random glassy matrix. The presence of  the  ODA part  of  the  repeat units is, therefore, believed to  be  principally responsible for  the  intersegmental spacing and intrasegmental  motions  useful for  gas  diffusion in  the  PMDA-ODA material.

texts.orig: subtle effect can  be  understood. The average number of  mobile  -Olinkages is decreased by  roughly 40%  per  unit volume  in  the  6FDA-ODA compared  to the  PMDA-ODA material. Along with  this reduction in  the  concentration of -Olinkages comes a simultaneous introduction of  the  mobility-restricted - C(CF3)2-  moiety, thereby increasing the concentration of  mobility restricted linkages in  the  polymer. Both  of  these factors make the  matrix more selective, and are  consistent with  the  observed  behavior for  these two polymers. These results illustrate the  application of  the  principles stated earlier for optimization of  the  properties of  a  given  family of  membrane materials. For  this principle to become truly quantitatively useful, one must have measures  of  the  relative disruptive effect and  relative segmental mobility around  flexible "joints" in  the solid state. The relative disruptive effect can  be characterized approximately using  X-ray  d-spacings shown in  Table  3,  but  relative mobility data  are  not currently available, so  the approach  is  still largely qualitative.

texts.orig: The permeabilities of  the  relatively larger penetrants such  as  N2  and CH  4 are  even  decreased due to  the  substitution of  6FDA for  the  PMDA, while those of the smaller penetrants such  as  He and  CO2 are increased. To understand such effects for  these  gases  having  small  differences in kinetic diameter, we  will  again  follow  considerations similar to those  used for  the  ODA polymer. Although the  PMDA section in  the  PMDA-IPDA polymer  is packable and  rigid, the  IPDA  section appears to  frustrate effective packing compared  to the  ODA section. This  is  seen  both  in  the  densities and d-spacings of  the PMDA-ODA vs  PMDA-IPDA materials shown in Table  3. This  "spacer effect" due  to  the IPDA group, along with  its somewhat  more  restricted motion  around this joint compared  to  the  - O - ,  satisfies the principle noted  earlier concerning the  value of  simultaneously introducing inhibition of  chain packing along  with inhibition of  intrasegmental motion. Structural changes  such  as  replacement of the  - O -   by  the  - C(CH3)2- which  tend to  open  the matrix, while at  the same time  increasing resistance to  motion  at  an  obvious  rotational joint may be  the most  effective means for  significantly increasing permeability without causing precipitous drops  in  selectivity. In the  substitution of  IPDA  for  ODA in  the PMDA series, sufficient rotational inhibition was not  introduced by  the  IPDA to  offset its general opening  of the  matrix, so  the  large increase in  permeability was accompanied  by a significant drop in  selectivity. Based on the  earlier discussion of the  PMDA-ODA and  6FDA-ODA pair, we can  see  that the 6FDA moiety  provides a  way around  this problem.

texts.orig: caused  a reduction in  the  concentration of  the  mobile -Olinkages due to  an  increase in  the  concentration of  -C(CF3)2-  linkages that have  a  more restricted rotational mobility. A similar case can  be  argued  for the  IPDA based polymer  where  the  PMDA linkage is  replaced with  a 6FDA  linkage. Table  8 shows an increase in  permeability for  6FDA-IPDA as  compared  to  PMDAIPDA; however, the magnitude of  this increase is not  as  large as  the permeability increase between  6FDA-ODA and  PMDA-ODA shown in  Table  3.  A possible reason for this moderation can  be  illustrated by  considering the  d-spacings for these  materials. Table  3 shows that by holding the  ODA link constant and replacing PMDA with  6FDA,  the  d-spacing increases by 1.0 A. On the  other hand,  Table  8 shows that by holding the  IPDA  link constant and replacing PMDA with  6FDA the  d-spacing increases by only  0.2A.  This  moderation occurs because  the  -C(CH3)2- and -C(CF3)  2linkages are  closer to  each other in  size and shape  than  are  the  - O and -C(CF3)  2moieties. As a result, the substitution f6FDA  for PMDA has  a  smaller "spacer" effect for the IPDA  material than  it has  in  the  ODA material. This  suggests that the  6FDA substitution has a smaller effective tendency  to  open the  already relatively packing resistant IPDA material compared  to  the more  compact  ODA  material.

texts.orig: Until  recently, it was thought  that the  highly flexible silicone rubber  polymer was the  most  permeable  and lowest selectivity material available for  gas  separations. Recent  data, however,  for  the  rigid chain  TMSP indicates that  it is  as much as  a factor of  ten  times  more permeable  with  a slightly lower  selectivity than  silicone rubber even  though  it is a  glassy polymer. One might  interpret the TMSP  data  as  evidence that the  glassy matrix  has  been  "opened"  so  extensively and the  backbone  motions  retarded so  effectively by  the  presence of  the  bulky trimethylsilyl substituent group  and  conjugated double  bonds  that the  material has  essentially ost its ability to  perform  size and  shape  selection. 62'63 The  marked difference between  the  mobility selectivity ofthe  substituted polycarbonates and this  material illustrate the  importance  not  only  of  achieving chain  rigidity, but  of  adjusting the  intersegmental packing  to  produce  useful selectivities at economically attractive permeability levels.

texts.orig: Extensive work by Paul  and coworkers  has  helped  to  define the  constraints that exist in  terms  of  finding miscible polymer  blends. 64-66 This  group  has  also defined  the  opportunities that  appear  to exist in terms  of optimization of membrane properties by  polymer  blending. Although  it has  not  been  shown in detail for  glassy  polymers,  it  is  generally believed  that  copolymerization, like  polymer blending, allows  one to  move  more or less  rationally between the  properties of  the  two constituent pure  components.  If  one could  achieve equivalent segmental compositions by  either blending or  copolymerization, it is currently thought that equivalent solution/diffusion separation properties would result. Because  of  the  general scarcity of  copolymer  data  for  interesting high  Tg materials, the following discussion will  focus  on the  blending  route  which is  a  more  economical  approach than copolymerization if  the constituent homopolymers are  miscible.

texts.orig: Paul and coworkers  have also  performed  extensive studies of  potential tradeoffs available for optimization of  membrane properties by  so-called "antiplasticization" due to  addition of  appropriately chosen  low  molecular weight additives (see Table  1 1). 69-7 1   The addition of  such  additives leads to  depression of  the  Tg  of  the  polymer, and this can  be  undesirable if the  entire membrane contains the  additive, since it undermines  its collapse strength. Moreover,  the potential loss of  the  antiplasticizing agent into the  external gas  stream is also a factor of  concern. If, however,  only  the  thin  dense  skin  of  an asymmetric structure could  be post-treated with  a low-migration tendency  material, the technique would  be  more attractive.

For materials such  as  polyvinyl alcohol and Amberplex,  this result is  presumably due to  the  high  degree of  swelling of  these materials, which are  on the  verge  of dissolving in  aqueous  environments. In  some cases these materials are  prevented from dissolving only  by the  presence of  crystallinity or  covalent crosslinks. In these cases, there is  essentially no mobility selectivity between  the  water  and  the salt. Moreover,  due to  the  high  degree  of  swelling, it is  likely that  the  average environment  experienced by a penetrant is  only  slightly different than that of water itself, so solubility regulation by the polymer matrix is also of minimal  importance. Clearly, too  much  swelling in  a potential RO  membrane is  undesirable. Introduction of  a controlled amount of  crosslinking provides a means  of restricting swelling  and providing  increased  selectivity with a tradeoff of  lower  water  permeability. This  principle has  been  applied successfully by FilmTec in  their high  flux, high  rejection thin  film  composite  FT-30 membrane.

texts.orig: Rather  different transport demands must  be  met  by  reverse osmosis  materials as  compared to  the  gas  separation materials that were  discussed previously. In the  case  of  gases, separation occurs  at  a  much smaller size scale (on  the  order of  fractions of  angstroms  in  size differences), and commercial  expectations of fluxes have  realistically accepted levels not  requiring plasticization or  swelling by the permeating  components.  The  size  selectivity requirements are considerably less demanding  in  RO  than  for  gases, especially if one  considers that a  hydration shell may significantly increase the  effective size of  the  ionic species. A  substantial fraction of  the  sorbed  water, on the  other hand,  is  free to  move essentially as a  more  compact monomeric  or slightly clustered species. Therefore, for  RO, transport through  a medium that has  diffusion coefficients on the  order  of  those  of  a highly plasticized polymer  leads to  extremely high water  permeation rates while at  the same time  maintaining rather impressive salt rejections. Nevertheless, even  with  the  thinnest dense  films that could  be  made without defects, Reid  and  Breton  found  that the  productivities were  rather low. The breakthrough  by Loeb and Sourirajan in  producing  very  thin  skinned asymmetric membranes based  on CA  opened  the  door  for commecially attractive  flux rates in  liquid and later gas  systems. 75'76

texts.orig: Membrane  research on hollow  fiber polyamide  RO  membranes in  the  early 1960s  led  to  the  introduction of  DuPont materials (B-5  permeator) based  on an aliphatic polyamide  in 1967. 78 Improved hollow  fiber modules based on aromatic polyamides (ARAMIDS) were  introduced by DuPont in  1969.  These materials resulted from  careful balancing of  structure levels 1  and 2,  discussed above,  using  mixed isomeric diamine  monomers  of  phenylenediamines with isophthalic and terephthalic diacid chlorides (Fig. 9).  Such an  approach  allows the  use  of  relatively inexpensive monomers with  excellent chain  stiffening abilities, while  avoiding crystallization in  the dope  or  in  the  permeable  skin as  would be the  case  for  the  pure  terephthalic or  isophthalic aramids.

Because  evolution of  material science for  RO  membranes has  already led  to  a trade off between  flux and selectivity properties of  the  basic membrane materials, additional complex  factors such  as durability, cost and  ease  of  fabrication are  currently important in  the  choice of the  specific material preferred. texts.orig: Several factors should  be  considered in  comparing  the  data  for  normalized water fluxes (P/l) in  Table  13.  Differences in  operating conditions, effective skin thickness and skin  morphologies caused  by variations in  casting protocols complicate comparisons of  the various entries. For  instance, effective skin thicknesses typically range  between  1000-2500/~,  representing a + 75%  uncertainty in  the calculated intrinsic permeability due  to  this factor alone. Therefore, some of  the  higher  reported flux  members may represent not  only  higher intrinsic permeabilities, but  also a  thinner skin. A more reliable evaluation of  structureproperty effects is  possible for  a homologous series of  materials where the membrane  formation protocols are presumably  sufficiently similar to give comparable  skin  thicknesses. For  example, the  polyamide  hydrazide structure shown in  Table  13 has  over  three times  the  water  transport capability as  the straight polyamide  with  essentially equivalent salt rejection. It is  believed that this difference represents a true  inherent permeability difference, since  the polyamide hydrazides are  open  matrices due  to  additional lowering of  the  ring symmetry,  and hence,  inhibited packing  ability due to the  different amine groups  in  the  1,3  positions on the  benzene  ring.

texts.orig: One would  like to  pursue the  above  issues of  structure-property relations for the  various materials in  Table 13 without  the  uncertainty in  effective skin thicknesses, however,  because  of  the  scarcity of  basic  sorption and transport data, this is not  really possible. It is  interesting that even  today, data  to  guide material selection for RO applications seem  to be  based more  on  direct measurements  of  flux and  rejection of  candidate materials in  asymmetric form  than  has been the  case  for  gas separations. There are  several reasons  for  this, but perhaps the  most  important cause  is the  revolutionary influence that the  LoebSourirajan discovery of  asymmetric  structures had on the  field at  its  early commercial stages. The  preliminary screening work  of Reid  and  Breton that was discussed earlier indicated that CA  was the  best  material for  RO  available at that time. Loeb  and  Sourirajan's discovery of  how to  make CA into useful, ultra high  flux form  reduced  early interest in  optimizing permeability vs  selectivity properties of  membrane  materials, since  optimization of  casting protocols would greatly overshadow  a 25-50%  higher inherent permeability of  a new material by  merely  reducing the  skin  thickness.

texts.orig: The critical importance  of  mobility factors discussed above does  not,  of course, mean that solubility issues can  be  neglected. In  addition to  their direct impact on  the solubility selectivity in(eq. 9), solubility factors can  also indirectly impact  the  mobility selectivity. For  instance, if one  modifies the  structure of  a homologous  series with  more hydrophilic groups  in  an  attempt to  increase the solubility selectivity, the mobility selectivity is likely to  be  reduced seriously. This is because the  material is likely to  swell more and  the  selectivity of  the  material will be compromised due to  an increase in  the  diffusion of  the  bulkier salt. Similar effects are  at play in  the case of  the family of  cellulose acetates. As noted earlier, decreased amounts  of  swelling accompany the  more highly acetylated CA's, and  this gives rise to  materials with higher permselectivity even  though  the partition coefficient for water is decreased as  the hydrophilicity of  the membrane drops.  Unfortunately, the  lack  of  published studies of  water  and salt partitioning and  diffusion coefficients in  the  many interesting materials in  Table  13 precludes the  discussion of  the  properties of  these materials in  terms  of  their basic transport properties.

texts.orig: It  appears  that the  main problems  in  RO  desalination currently are  not primarily ones  of  achieving adequate  salt rejection or  water  flux, but  rather achieving the  ability to  operate under  demanding higher  temperatures and transmembrane  pressures and improving  resistance to  biological or  chemical degradation. These  later requirements are  not  simply  related to  the  transport and sorption phenomena inside the  membrane. This  fact, is  probably  also responsible for  the  focus  of  current research on the  integrated membrane support and  skin structure and  its durability and  fouling resistance rather than on the  fundamental transport properties of  the  selective skin.

texts.orig: The chlorine resistance of  the  materials in  the  above  Table  13 is  a point  of interest, since it  would be attractive to  avoid  the  necessity of  pretreatment to remove chlorine. Although  CA  is  not  as  selective as  the  aromatic materials in Table 13,  it  can tolerate chlorine at 1-2  ppm  for  extended  periods in  the  pH range  from 6-8.  For the  nitrogen-containing polymers,  degradation due to chlorine attack is  more serious. Originally, it was thought  that  direct chlorine attack on the  H's  in  the  secondary amides of  the  backbone  was the  primary process occurring; however,  the  possibility exists that electrophilic substitution occurs on  the  aromatic rings 98"~°5 followed by  subsequent disruption of  hydrogen bonding  or  even  hydrolysis of  the  neighboring amide groups.

texts.orig: In a practical sense,  chlorine susceptibility has not been a very serious problem preventing the  use of  the  materials in Table 13, since  the  cost  of dechlorination is  modest  and these materials typically have  a higher salt rejection  and are  not  as  susceptible to  biological attack. The dechlorination cost is  primarily associated with  the  addition of  sodium bisulfite or  sodium thiosulfate 28 with  subsequent  treatment with  NaOH to  produce  sodium chloride which is  subsequently removed in  the  desalination process.

texts.orig: Thin  film  composites, such  as  the  polyamide  skinned  membranes referred to in  Table  13  have  both  high  flux and highly selective interfacially formed  layers supported  on a polysulfone support. The inherent salt and water  transport properties of  the  materials are  probably similar to  those of  the  integrally skinned polyamides and  polyamide-hydrazides also  discussed in  Table  13.  Also,  there is some evidence that crosslinking introduced through  the  use  of  materials such  as trimesyl chloride provides improved  resistance to  chain  scission by chlorine. A composite  sulfonate ester  crosslinked membrane  on  a  porous polysulfone support  has been noted  for  resistance to 100ppm  chlorine over  a period  of 5000h at  a flux of  (1  gfd), i.e. 4.7 x  l0 5gm/(cm2sec)  and 98%  rejection level for  3500  ppm  brackish water  at  400  psia. 26  The data  on chlorine resistance for all membrane materials are  somewhat ambiguous,  and  more careful work needs to be done in this  area  to understand  the  effects of short-term accidental exposure  of  membranes caused  by upsets  in  the  dechlorination process. The polyethercyanurate composite  membrane  discussed in Table 13 is  extremely sensitive to  oxidation by chlorine or  even  dissolved oxygen;  however,  addition of  excess  sodium bisulfite suppresses this problem  effectively in  practice.

texts.orig: Highly  selective new membranes similar to  the  crosslinked PEC-1000 may offer a potential means of  extending the  scope  of  RO  applications to  include solution concentration as  a complementary  process to  reduce  energy  costs  in recovery  of valuable  or toxic  organics  from aqueous streams.  The  reverse osmosis  process is, however,  generally not  a very  attractive means of  concentrating small  organic  molecules, since  large  osmotic  pressures must be overcome. Moreover,  if the  membranes sorb  significant amounts of  the  chemicals and are  plasticized, the  ability to  withstand large transmembrane  pressures of say  1000  psi  may be  poor, so  this application is likely to  require more advanced materials. Recently, however,  it has  been  reported that a successful 300  ton/day plant  to recover  e-caprolactam  is in operation  based on  the PEC-1000 material.~°7

texts.orig: One can avoid  the  need to  apply  large  pressures to  overcome the  osmotic pressure difference in  standard reverse osmosis  operations by pulling a vacuum on the  downstream face  of  the  membrane. This  process is  typically referred to as "pervaporation" to  reflect  he  need for  both  permeation  and evaporation processes to  occur.  Pervaporation provides the  maximum chemical  potential drop  to  drive diffusion; however,  it necessitates the  input of  energy  to  vaporize the  permeated  component at  the  downstream membrane face. 32'1°8 If  the  feed stream  can  be  fed  as  a saturated vapor, e.g. from  the  overhead  of  a distillation column such  energy  requirements can be moderated.

Pervaporation provides the  maximum chemical  potential drop  to  drive diffusion; however,  it necessitates the  input of  energy  to  vaporize the  permeated  component at  the  downstream membrane face. 32'1°8 If  the  feed stream  can  be  fed  as  a saturated vapor, e.g. from  the  overhead  of  a distillation column such  energy  requirements can be moderated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While the  feeding of  a saturated vapor  eliminates the  energy  supply  step, it generally means that  the feed  will  be supplied  at an elevated temperature, thereby placing unusual  demands on  the  solvent resistance of  the  membrane and module adhesives  and seals. There continues to be considerable interest in the approach to either replace  or assist conventional separations, such as distillation, with  pervaporation separations because  it  could  offer a solution for  an even more  diverse  range of separation applications than for  gases. Nevertheless, the  number of  actual applications of  the  technology remain  rather small, because  of  the  lack  of  appropriate materials. Durability of  the  membrane material  and module components present  a bigger  challenge than for  gas separations. The membrane durability issue will likely be  dealt with  ultimately using either  physical  or chemical crosslinking; however, basic transport property issues which are  the  subject of  this review  must also  be resolved to move this field forward  significantly.

texts.orig: The precise scale of  size differences required for achieving adequate mobility selectivity in  pervaporation applications can  only be  defined when one  considers specific systems, and these issues have  not  yet  been  treated even  for the  most common  systems such  as  alcohols and  water. Generally, discussions of  material optimization in  this area are  based  largely on  solubility effects. 1°9 As in  the case of  RO, it is generally felt  hat economically attractive fluxes can  be  achieved best when the membrane is partially or  highly plasticized. In  this situation, of course, solubility effects interact with  mobility effects; and the  characterization of sorption and  transport properties of  penetrants must  be  done  under mixed  feed conditions.

Recently, copolymers  and blends with  varying hydrogen  bonding  interaction abilities have  been  considered for alcohol-water separations. 11 1 - 1 1 3 Moreover,  a recent study has  considered the  use  of  aromatic polyamides from  RO  work for the  pervaporation application. ~ 1 4   If a sufficiently thin skin  of  a  robust, highly selective but "open"  structure could  be generated, lower  sorbing, mobility selective polymers packaged  in  hollow  fiber or  spiral wound forms  may indeed be  desirable alternatives to consider. On the  other  hand,  the  introduction of  unintentional orientation, stresses and significant differences in  skin  defect levels between  different preparations of asymmetric membranes based  on  even  the same polymer  are  possible. This  fact alone  suggests that when considering new materials for  advanced  membrane development the  first step should be  unambiguous  sorption and  transport comparisons of  the  candidate materials. Since  permselection is a  molecularly based phenomenon,  the  cleaner the  comparison between  the  molecular capability of two materials to  perform  subtle thermodynamic  and kinetic discriminations between  selected penetrants, the  more rational will be  conclusions concerning structure-property relationships. One must  be  careful, of  course, not  to  become bogged  down in  the  initial work  with dense  films, if the  aim  is to  ultimately rely upon  asymmetric structures. As  in  the RO case, the next step beyond  dense  films requires decisions about  whether  integral or  composite formation approaches should  be used. This  aspect of  the  problem  is  not  primary  to  our  discussion here, but  has  been  treated by several authors for  different types of  membrane materials.98, ll6-118

texts.orig: For  these highly swollen materials, some of  the penetrants with  hydrodynamic sizes less than  that of  the  swollen mesh size of  the  membrane move through it  with  little hindrance. Significantly arger penetrants, however,  undergo  a hindered diffusion and/or hydrodynamic sieving process. This  situation clearly eliminates the  problem  of  overcoming  high  osmotic pressures due  to  dissolved salts and low molecular  weight  organics. It  also  offers the  possibility of "desalting" or  "dealcoholizing" the  nonpermeate stream, which  in  many cases may be a food, where  losing desirable flavor or  nutrient components  such  as sugars is  not  tolerable. Again,  the  approach  typically used  involves only  the measurement  of  flux and  selectivity rather than  being  concerned with  tailoring sorption and diffusion properties specifically. Given the  complex transition nature of  the transport phenomena  occurring, this may be  necessary for the time being; however,  it would  be  a  natural area  for  fundamental investigations.

texts.orig: Food processing, of  course  offers special challenges because,  the  difficultto-handle components cannot  be removed using  simple  procedures  as was described for  the  case  of  chlorine in  water. Components that attack or  foul  the membrane must  be  dealt with  in  a  more sophisticated fashion on a  case-by-case basis. Temperature  ranges also must  be  extended upward to  include conditions near  processing conditions for  foods. This  minimizes  bacterial contamination and simplifies handling  procedures. Toxicity considerations are  issues to be dealt with  in  developing new materials for  such  applications, since  the  FDA must be convinced  of  the  innocuous nature  of  any  residual components  or  of effective post-formation protocols for  removal of  questionable components such  as  aromatic diamines  and related compounds.

texts.orig: Early synthetic ion-containing materials for membranes tended to  be  derived by modification of  simple  resins such  as  polystyrene and polysulfone. For instance, polystyrene crosslinked with  small  amounts of  divinyl benzene  to prevent uncontrolled swelling or  dissolution can  be  prepared as  either a cation exchange  or  an  anion exchange  material. The  structures, hown in  Table  18  are achieved by  treating the  crosslinked resin via  sulfonation (cation exchanger) or chloromethylating and replacing the  chlorines with trimethylamine (anion exchanger). Clearly, the  cation exchanger  has fixed  anions  on  the  chain [-SO~-]  with  mobile  cations while  the  anion  exchanger  has fixed  cations [-  +N(CH3)3] with  mobile  anions. 98  The  sodium  salt of  the  sulfonated material is used  in  water  softeners as  swollen gel  beads  to  remove  calcium, followed by regeneration simply  using  backflushing with  an excess  of  sodium chloride solution to  regenerate the  material.

texts.orig: If  one places  an ion  exchange  material in  a dilute solution of  a strong electrolyte, an  effect known as  coion  exclusion results. As illustrated in  Fig. I  1 for a  cation exchange  case, the  larger concentration of  cations in  the  membrane compared  to  the  dilute external phase  initially establishes a  gradient that tends to  drive cations out  of  the  membrane and into the  external solution, thereby establishing a net  positive charge  in  the  external solution and a net  negative charge in  the membrane.  This  situation leads to  a  so-called "Donnan  Potential" that opposes  further charge separation that would  result from  additional movement  of cations from  the membrane into the external solution. At  the same  time, the  slightly net  negative charge  on the  membrane that balances the  positive charge  in  the  solution serves  to  repel  the  mobile  anions  and suppress their partitioning into the  membrane.  This  leads to  the  so-called "coion exclusion", in  which  mobile  anions are  excluded by  fixed anions in  cation exchange  membranes  and  vice  versa for  mobile  cations in  anion  exchange  membranes.

texts.orig: The  individual point charge nature of  the fixed charges become  most  apparent and causes  breakdown of  the  simple  theory in  at  least two easily visualized limiting cases. In  one  case,  if the  membrane has  only  a low number of  fixed charges the  ion  groups  are  effectively isolated from  each  other, thereby accentuating their point charge  natures. In  the  second  case, in  the  presence of  high concentrations of  sorbed counterions, and  at high  ionic strengths relative to  the fixed charge concentration, effective shielding of  the fixed ion  groups from  each other can  occur. This  tends to  again  preclude the  establishment of  an  effective uniform field in  the polymer  and  accentuates the point charge nature of  the fixed ions.

texts.orig: Since  the morphology  of  Nation ® Perfluorinated Membranes tends to  give  a very  low effective diffusion coefficient for  the  coion  (C1-), and a fairly high effective diffusion coefficient for  the  counterion (Na+), the membrane  is extremely effective in  chlor-alkali cells where  precisely this requirement is at  a premium.  Therefore, along with  its excellent resistance to  the aggressive stream components, Nation ® Perfluorinated Membranes offers unique  permselection properties due to  its higher  order  structuring of  the  water  phase  within  its confines. In  essence, Nation ® Perfluorinated Membranes represents an  example of  a  new generation of  tailoring of  membrane properties that allows significant decoupling of  the  movements of  two species. It provides a  clearly documented example of  a material in  which an additional structure level, such  as  those alluded to  in  the  introduction of  this review, has  been  incorporated into  the membrane  to  provide  additional control of  selectivity. This  is, indeed, the beauty of  the  membrane approach, whose ultimate potential is limited only  by the  ability to  conceive and execute  molecular scale  control of  motions  and morphologies.
